title: Single-Ensemble Multiparameter Squeezing with Qudits
authors: Xiaoshui Lin, Chunlei Qu, Chong Zu, Chuanwei Zhang
abstract: Quantum-enhanced multiparameter sensing is often associated with distributed architectures or 2-anticoherent states, whereas squeezing in a single collective ensemble is typically limited to single-parameter metrology. Here, we show that a single ensemble can support simultaneous multiparameter squeezing when each sensor is promoted from a qubit to a qudit (i.e., spin with $d$ energy levels). We develop a general framework in which the optimal product probe state, the corresponding global readout observables, and the associated squeezing parameters are all determined from the single-site quantum Fisher information matrix. We then present a minimal qudit construction for two-parameter vector magnetic field sensing with local dimension $d=3$. We further identify a collective twisting-like interacting Hamiltonian that generates such multiparameter-squeezed states and numerically demonstrate scalable metrological gain. In particular, for a trapped-ion qutrit chain with power-law interactions, we obtain up to 12 dB enhancement in two-parameter sensing for $N=256$ sensors. Our results establish qudit-enabled multiparameter squeezing in a single ensemble as a distinct route to multiparameter quantum metrology with global readout, and highlight its potential advantage over distributed multi-ensemble strategies in the fixed-sensor-budget regime.
summary: The paper shows that using qudits instead of qubits allows a single ensemble to perform simultaneous multi-parameter sensing. It establishes a mathematical framework for this effect and demonstrates significant metrological gain in a simulated trapped-ion system.
main_problem: Achieving simultaneous multiparameter sensing (e.g., vector magnetic field detection) within a single collective ensemble, which is typically limited to single-parameter metrology in qubit-based systems.
main_result: By promoting local sensors from qubits to qudits, the authors demonstrate that a single ensemble can support multiparameter squeezing, achieving up to 12 dB enhancement in a trapped-ion qutrit chain.
method: A general framework based on the single-site Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM) to determine optimal probe states and readout observables, combined with Discrete Truncated Wigner Approximation (DTWA) for numerical simulations.
model_or_system: A single ensemble of N identical d-level qudit sensors, specifically implemented as a trapped-ion qutrit chain with power-law interactions and a collective twisting-like interacting Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Multiparameter squeezing parameter (xi^2), collective spin operators (S_alpha), Symmetric Logarithmic Derivatives (SLD), and transduction-rate operator matrix (G_alpha_beta).
important_parameters: Local dimension (d), number of sensors (N), power-law interaction exponent (gamma), and the Standard Quantum Limit (SQL).
main_assumptions: The ensemble is initially in a direct product state; the optimization assumes the ability to implement optimal SLD measurements; the framework is subject to the Matsumoto weak-commutativity theorem.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) provides a schematic of qudits and their phase space using SU(d) generators, illustrating how multiple independent response channels can coexist.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the limitations of qubit-based sensing, proposes a qudit-based solution, develops a mathematical framework using QFIM, demonstrates a concrete qutrit implementation, and provides numerical evidence using a trapped-ion model.
why_interesting: It provides a new route to multiparameter metrology by expanding the local Hilbert space, offering a distinct advantage over distributed architectures in the fixed-sensor-budget regime, which is highly relevant for quantum sensing and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of qudits and their phase space formed by the collective traceless SU(d) generators {Λ1, Λ2, . . . , Λd2−1}. With a total of d2 −1 collective operators, we can encode multi-phase information in multiple subspaces. The SU(d) coherent state lives in the complex projective space of dimension d −1, denoted as CPd−1. (b) Schematic of the squeezing and sensing process via qudits. The initial state is prepared into an optimized coherent state |ψp⟩= |ϕ⟩⊗N. Then, an interacting Hamilto- nian simultaneously twists the initial state across multiple subspaces, thereby achieving metrological gain. After the squeezing state preparation, the encoding operator Uθ = e−i P α θαSα...

FIG. 2. (a) (b) Dynamics of measurement operators lx and ly under the in-plane magnetic field from initial state |ψs⟩= |0⟩. The red dots denote linear detection based on changes in the expectations of lx and ly. Here, we set Bx/By = 0.1, 0.5, 1.0 in (a); and set Bx/By = 0.1, 0.4, 0.8 in (b). (c) Dynamics of the squeezing parameter ξ2 in under the interacting Hamil- tonian with different qudit number N. The black dots denote the optimal squeezing parameter (ξ2 in)op and the correspond- ing time top. (d) The scaling relation of the optimal squeezing parameter (ξ2 in)op versus the qudit number N. The numerical fitting yields (ξ2 in)op ∼N −k with k ≃0.94.

FIG. 3. (a) The 171Yb+ ions are trapped in a one-dimensional Paul trap with transverse phonon modes. Three lasers form a beating pattern to mediate the spin-spin interaction. (b) Three hyperfine states in the ground-state manifold 2S1/2 form the d = 3 qudit. (c) Squeezing parameter ξ2 in vs. evolu- tion time J0t at different system size. We set V0/J0 = 2, and this does not dramatically change the optimal squeezing pa- rameter. (d) The fitting exponent k of the optimal squeezing parameter to the qudit number vs. interaction decay expo- nent γ. The dashed dotted line denotes the OAT results, and the dashed line denotes the SQL with no metrological gain.
